title: Branching & Merging
description: Learn how to create branches, switch between them, and merge changes back together.

sections.heading: What is a Branch?
sections.content: A branch is a lightweight movable pointer to a commit. It allows you to work on features or fixes in isolation without affecting the main codebase.

sections.heading: Creating and Switching Branches
sections.content: Create a new branch with `git branch <branch-name>` and switch to it with `git checkout <branch-name>` or `git switch <branch-name>`.

sections.heading: Merging Branches
sections.content: Merge changes from one branch into another with `git merge <branch-name>`. Git will automatically combine the changes if there are no conflicts.

sections.heading: Resolving Merge Conflicts
sections.content: When the same lines are modified in both branches, Git can't auto-merge. You must manually resolve conflicts in the affected files, then `git add` and `git commit`.

quiz.question: Which command creates a new branch?
quiz.explanation: All three commands can create a branch: `git branch` creates, `git checkout -b` creates and switches, `git switch -c` does the same.

quiz.question: How do you switch to an existing branch?
quiz.explanation: Both `git checkout` and `git switch` can switch branches.

quiz.question: What does `git merge feature` do?
quiz.explanation: `git merge feature` takes the changes from the 'feature' branch and merges them into your current branch.

quiz.question: What causes a merge conflict?
options: Modifying the same lines in two branches
quiz.explanation: Merge conflicts occur when the same part of a file is modified in both branches being merged.

quiz.question: How do you resolve a merge conflict?
options: Edit the file manually, then git add and commit
Abort the merge with git merge --abort
quiz.explanation: To resolve a conflict, manually edit the conflicted file to keep the desired changes, then `git add` and `git commit`.
